examples.title: Pseudocode: Linear List and Hash Table Directory Operations
examples.description: Demonstrates both linear list and hash table approaches for directory operations including search, insert, and delete
examples.code:
ALGORITHM LinearListDirectorySearch(directory, targetFileName)
INPUT: directory (linear list), targetFileName
OUTPUT: pointer to FCB/inode or NULL if not found

BEGIN
 FOR each entry IN directory DO
 IF entry.fileName == targetFileName THEN
 RETURN entry.fcbPointer
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN NULL
END

ALGORITHM LinearListDirectoryInsert(directory, newFileName, fcbPointer)
INPUT: directory, newFileName, fcbPointer
OUTPUT: TRUE if successful, FALSE if duplicate

BEGIN
 # Check for duplicate name
 IF LinearListDirectorySearch(directory, newFileName) != NULL THEN
 RETURN FALSE # File already exists
 END IF
 # Add new entry at end
 newEntry.fileName = newFileName
 newEntry.fcbPointer = fcbPointer
 APPEND newEntry TO directory
 RETURN TRUE
END

ALGORITHM HashTableDirectorySearch(directoryTable, hashFunction, targetFileName)
INPUT: directoryTable (hash table), hashFunction, targetFileName
OUTPUT: pointer to FCB/inode or NULL if not found

BEGIN
 index = hashFunction(targetFileName)
 chain = directoryTable[index]
 
 FOR each entry IN chain DO
 IF entry.fileName == targetFileName THEN
 RETURN entry.fcbPointer
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN NULL
END

ALGORITHM HashTableDirectoryInsert(directoryTable, hashFunction, newFileName, fcbPointer)
INPUT: directoryTable, hashFunction, newFileName, fcbPointer
OUTPUT: TRUE if successful, FALSE if duplicate

BEGIN
 index = hashFunction(newFileName)
 chain = directoryTable[index]
 
 # Check for duplicate in chain
 FOR each entry IN chain DO
 IF entry.fileName == newFileName THEN
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 # Add new entry to the chain (chaining for collision handling)
 newEntry.fileName = newFileName
 newEntry.fcbPointer = fcbPointer
 APPEND newEntry TO chain
 RETURN TRUE
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows two directory implementation approaches: 1) Linear List - uses sequential search O(n) for all operations, 2) Hash Table - uses hash function to compute index, then searches the collision chain. Both algorithms check for duplicate names before insertion to maintain file name uniqueness.

examples.title: Python: Directory Implementation with Linear List and Hash Table
examples.description: Complete Python implementation demonstrating both linear list and hash table directory structures with operations
examples.code:
class LinearListDirectory:
 """Directory implemented as a linear list"""
 
 def __init__(self):
 self.entries = [] # List of (filename, inode_pointer) tuples
 
 def search(self, filename):
 """Search for a file - O(n) time complexity"""
 for name, inode in self.entries:
 if name == filename:
 return inode
 return None
 
 def insert(self, filename, inode):
 """Insert new file - O(n) for duplicate check"""
 # Check for duplicate
 if self.search(filename) is not None:
 return False # File already exists
 self.entries.append((filename, inode))
 return True
 
 def delete(self, filename):
 """Delete a file - O(n) search"""
 for i, (name, inode) in enumerate(self.entries):
 if name == filename:
 self.entries.pop(i)
 return True
 return False
 
 def list_all(self):
 """List all files - O(n)"""
 return [name for name, _ in self.entries]


class HashTableDirectory:
 """Directory implemented as a hash table with chaining"""
 
 def __init__(self, table_size=16):
 self.table_size = table_size
 self.table = [[] for _ in range(table_size)] # Array of lists (chains)
 
 def _hash(self, filename):
 """Simple hash function - sum of character codes"""
 return sum(ord(c) for c in filename) % self.table_size
 
 def search(self, filename):
 """Search for a file - O(1) average, O(n) worst case"""
 index = self._hash(filename)
 chain = self.table[index]
 
 for name, inode in chain:
 if name == filename:
 return inode
 return None
 
 def insert(self, filename, inode):
 """Insert new file - O(1) average"""
 # Check for duplicate
 if self.search(filename) is not None:
 return False
 
 index = self._hash(filename)
 self.table[index].append((filename, inode))
 return True
 
 def delete(self, filename):
 """Delete a file - O(1) average"""
 index = self._hash(filename)
 chain = self.table[index]
 
 for i, (name, _) in enumerate(chain):
 if name == filename:
 chain.pop(i)
 return True
 return False
 
 def list_all(self):
 """List all files - O(n)"""
 all_files = []
 for chain in self.table:
 all_files.extend(name for name, _ in chain)
 return all_files
 
 def show_structure(self):
 """Display hash table structure for debugging"""
 for i, chain in enumerate(self.table):
 print(f"Index {i}: {[(n, ino) for n, ino in chain]}")


# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
 print("=" * 50)
 print("Linear List Directory Demo")
 print("=" * 50)
 linear_dir = LinearListDirectory()
 
 linear_dir.insert("report.txt", 42)
 linear_dir.insert("main.c", 17)
 linear_dir.insert("data.csv", 88)
 
 print(f"Files: {linear_dir.list_all()}")
 print(f"Search 'main.c': inode {linear_dir.search('main.c')}")
 linear_dir.delete("main.c")
 print(f"After delete: {linear_dir.list_all()}")
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 50)
 print("Hash Table Directory Demo")
 print("=" * 50)
 hash_dir = HashTableDirectory(table_size=10)
 
 hash_dir.insert("report.txt", 42)
 hash_dir.insert("main.c", 17)
 hash_dir.insert("data.csv", 88)
 hash_dir.insert("notes.txt", 29)
 
 print(f"Files: {hash_dir.list_all()}")
 print(f"Search 'data.csv': inode {hash_dir.search('data.csv')}")
 print("\nHash table structure:")
 hash_dir.show_structure()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation shows both directory approaches. LinearListDirectory uses a simple list with O(n) search, while HashTableDirectory uses an array of linked lists (chaining) for collision handling with O(1) average search time. The hash function sums character codes for demonstration purposes.
examples.timeComplexity: Linear List: O(n) for all ops | Hash Table: O(1) avg, O(n) worst
examples.spaceComplexity: Linear List: O(n) | Hash Table: O(n + table_size)

examples.title: C: Hash Table Directory Implementation with Chaining
examples.description: C implementation of directory using hash table with collision handling through chaining
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates a hash table directory with chaining for collision handling. Key features: 1) Hash function uses polynomial rolling hash for better distribution, 2) Chaining handles collisions by storing multiple entries at each index, 3) All operations (search, insert, delete) have O(1) average time complexity, 4) Memory is dynamically allocated for entries and properly freed.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) average for all operations, O(n) worst case when all keys hash to same index
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n + TABLE_SIZE) where n is number of files
